title: Air Fryer Fried Chicken
tagline: Buttermilk-marinated crispy fried chicken with none of the deep-fry guilt.
macroNotes: Estimated for 500 g thigh + coating. Air-frying instead of deep-frying already saves you a few hundred calories versus classic fried chicken.
Boneless skinless breast instead of thigh drops fat by ~10–15 g per serving for the same protein — but it's less forgiving, so cut it into even 2–2.5 cm pieces and watch the timing.
Protein is already excellent here, so no soya needed. To lean it out further, use breast and go light on the oil spray (just enough to lose the raw-flour look).
Serve with a protein parotta and a salad rather than two regular parottas to keep it a genuine cutting meal.

ingredients.group: Buttermilk marinade (500 g boneless chicken thigh — best — or breast)
items: 3/4 cup buttermilk / chaach (or 1/2 cup thick curd + 2–3 tbsp water; or 3/4 cup milk + 1 tbsp lemon/vinegar rested 5 min)
2 tbsp thick curd (key for body so the coating grips)
1 tsp salt (skip if using salted/masala chaach)
1/2 tsp salt (halve it if the marinade was salty)
Optional: 1/2 tsp garlic powder, 2 tbsp crushed cornflakes/breadcrumbs
Oil spray (mandatory — air fryers bake, they don't fry)

ingredients.group: Kerala-style option (instead of the coating spices above)
items: 1 small onion, thinly sliced, and lime wedges to finish

steps.title: Marinate
steps.body: Mix 3/4 cup buttermilk/chaach with 2 tbsp thick curd, 1 tsp salt, 1 tsp chilli powder, 1/2 tsp pepper, 1/2 tsp garam masala and 1/2 tsp ginger-garlic paste. Coat 500 g chicken, cover, and refrigerate — minimum 2 hours, ideal 4–6, max 10–12. This mild-acid soak is what replaces the moisture a deep-fry would give, so don't skip it. Using masala chaach? Add NO extra salt and taste a drop of the marinade before coating.

steps.title: Mix the coating
steps.body: Whisk 1/2 cup maida, 2 tbsp cornflour, 1/2 tsp salt, 1/2 tsp pepper and 1/2 tsp paprika (plus garlic powder / crushed cornflakes if using). Keep the coating salt low if the marinade was salty. For the Kerala version, skip the paprika and instead plan to fry curry leaves + 1 tsp cracked pepper into the finish.

steps.title: Bread the chicken
steps.body: Take the chicken straight from the fridge and press each piece firmly into the dry mix — press it on, don't shake it off. Rest the breaded pieces 5 minutes so the crust sets.

steps.title: Oil the surface — critical
steps.body: Spray or brush oil over every piece until the coating looks slightly glossy, not matte. Dry flour will never crisp — this is the single most common failure point.

steps.title: Stage 1 — cook the meat
steps.body: Preheat the air fryer to 180°C for 5 min. Arrange the chicken in a single layer, not touching. Cook at 180°C: 7 min, then flip and spray oil, then 6 min more. The inside turns opaque here.

steps.title: Stage 2 — set and crisp the crust
steps.body: Turn it up to 200°C, give another light oil spray, and cook 3 min, flip, 2 min more. Air-fryer fried chicken is TWO temperatures / two phases — 'white inside + golden outside' is not done; you're waiting for the crust. Stop when no white flour patches remain and the surface is dry, rough and golden — don't push it dark or the meat dries.

steps.title: Rest
steps.body: Rest 3 minutes so the juices settle.

steps.title: Finish / Kerala-style option
steps.body: Classic: dust with chaat masala and a squeeze of lemon, serve with garlic mayo or peri-peri. Kerala-style: fry a handful of curry leaves and 1 tsp cracked pepper in a little oil, toss the fried chicken through it with thin onion slices, and finish with lime.

notes: Chicken frozen solid and no time to thaw? Cold-water thaw in a leak-proof bag (change water every 20 min), or microwave on DEFROST only 30–60 sec just to separate the pieces — never until soft. Then cut thin, marinate, and refrigerate. Never marinate a rock-solid block; the marinade can't penetrate.

If your first batch comes out pale with raw flour patches, it's a crust problem, not a safety one: spray oil over the floury spots and blast at 200°C for 5–6 min (3 min a side), stopping the moment the flour disappears.
Cook in batches rather than stacking — overcrowding steams the coating and it won't crisp.
source: Adapted from my own cooking notes.
